The **CAF Champions League**, known for sponsorship purposes as the **[TotalEnergies](/wiki/TotalEnergies "TotalEnergies") CAF Champions League**[[1]](#cite_note-1) and formerly the **African Cup of Champions Clubs**, is an annual [club football](/wiki/Football_club_(association_football) "Football club (association football)") competition organized by the [Confederation of African Football](/wiki/Confederation_of_African_Football "Confederation of African Football") (CAF) and contested by [top-division African clubs](/wiki/List_of_top-division_football_clubs_in_CAF_countries "List of top-division football clubs in CAF countries"), deciding the competition winners through a round robin group stage to qualify for a double-legged knockout stage, and then a home and away final. It is the most prestigious club competition in African football.

The winner of each season of the competition earns a berth for the [FIFA Club World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Club_World_Cup "FIFA Club World Cup"), a tournament contested between the champion clubs from all six continental confederations, faces the winner of the [CAF Confederation Cup](/wiki/CAF_Confederation_Cup "CAF Confederation Cup") in the following season's [CAF Super Cup](/wiki/CAF_Super_Cup "CAF Super Cup") and from 2024 onwards, along with the next 4 best teams, a place in the new [FIFA Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Intercontinental_Cup "FIFA Intercontinental Cup"). Clubs that finish as runners-up their national leagues, having not qualified for the Champions League, are eligible for the second-tier [CAF Confederation Cup](/wiki/CAF_Confederation_Cup "CAF Confederation Cup").

[Egyptian](/wiki/Egyptian_Football_Association "Egyptian Football Association") clubs have the highest number of victories (19 titles), followed by [Morocco](/wiki/Royal_Moroccan_Football_Federation "Royal Moroccan Football Federation") with 7. Egypt also has the largest number of winning teams, with four clubs having won the title. The competition has been won by 26 clubs, 12 of which have won it more than once. [Al Ahly](/wiki/Al_Ahly_SC "Al Ahly SC") is the most successful club in the competition's history, having it a record 12 times. [Pyramids FC](/wiki/Pyramids_FC "Pyramids FC") are the current African champions, having beaten [Mamelodi Sundowns F.C.](/wiki/Mamelodi_Sundowns_F.C. "Mamelodi Sundowns F.C.") 3–2 on aggregate in the [2025 final](/wiki/2025_CAF_Champions_League_final "2025 CAF Champions League final").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Champions_League&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Caf_Champions_League_1964-1977.png/250px-Caf_Champions_League_1964-1977.png)](/wiki/File:Caf_Champions_League_1964-1977.png)

First-ever trophy, designed in 1964 (used until 1977).

Established in 1964 as the ***African Cup of Champions Clubs***, the first team to lift the trophy was [Cameroonian](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon") team [Oryx Douala](/wiki/Oryx_Douala "Oryx Douala") who beat [Stade Malien](/wiki/Stade_Malien "Stade Malien") of [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali") 2–1 in a one-off final.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The 1966 edition introduced the two-legged 'home and away' final, which saw another Malian team [AS Real Bamako](/wiki/AS_Real_Bamako "AS Real Bamako") take on [Stade d'Abidjan](/wiki/Stade_d%27Abidjan "Stade d'Abidjan") of [Ivory Coast](/wiki/Ivory_Coast "Ivory Coast"). Real Bamako won the home leg 3–1 but it all came apart for them in the away game in Abidjan as the Ivorians went on to win 4–1 to take the title 5–4 on aggregate.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In 1967 when [Asante Kotoko](/wiki/Asante_Kotoko_S.C. "Asante Kotoko S.C.") of Ghana met [TP Mazembe](/wiki/TP_Mazembe "TP Mazembe") of the Democratic Republic of the Congo (or the DRC for short), both matches ended in draws (1–1 and 2–2 respectively). CAF arranged a play-off, but Kotoko failed to appear[[4]](#cite_note-4) and the title was handed to Mazembe, who went on to win the title again the following year.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

However, the Ghanaians got their revenge in 1970, when Kotoko and Mazembe once again met in the final. Once again, the first game ended 1–1, but against expectation, the Ghanaians ran out 2–1 winners in their away game to lift the title that had eluded them three years earlier.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The 1970s saw a remarkable rise in the fortunes of Cameroonian club football, which created the platform of success enjoyed by [Cameroonian](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon") football at international level today.

Between 1971 and 1980 Cameroonian teams won the cup four times, with [Canon Yaoundé](/wiki/Canon_Yaound%C3%A9 "Canon Yaoundé") taking three titles (1971,[[7]](#cite_note-7) 1978[[8]](#cite_note-8) and 1980[[9]](#cite_note-9)) and [US Douala](/wiki/Union_Douala "Union Douala") lifting the cup in 1979. In between the Cameroonian victories the honor was shared with another team enjoying a golden age, Guinean side [Hafia Conakry](/wiki/Hafia_Football_Club "Hafia Football Club"), who won it three times during this period (1972,[[10]](#cite_note-10) 1975[[11]](#cite_note-11) and 1977[[12]](#cite_note-12))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Caf_Champions_League_1978-1993.png/120px-Caf_Champions_League_1978-1993.png)](/wiki/File:Caf_Champions_League_1978-1993.png)

Second trophy, in use from 1977 to 1993.

### 1997–present: Change of name and rise in reputation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Champions_League&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1997–present: Change of name and rise in reputation")]

Apart from the introduction of the [away goals rule](/wiki/Away_goals_rule "Away goals rule"), very little changed in this competition until 1997, when CAF under [Issa Hayatou](/wiki/Issa_Hayatou "Issa Hayatou") took the bold step to follow the lead established a few years earlier by UEFA by creating a league/group stage in the tournament and changing the name to the **CAF Champions League** (in line with UEFA's [own Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League")). CAF also introduced prize money for participants for the first time with the initial offering of US$1 million to the winners and US$750,000 to the runners-up, making the rebranded competition the richest African club competition at the time.

In the new format, the league champions of the respective CAF member countries go through a series of qualification rounds until a round of 16 stage. The 8 winners are then drawn into two groups of 4 teams each, with each team playing each other on a home and away basis. At the end of the league stage, the top team in each group met in the final, in two-legged games (home and away). In [the 2001 season](/wiki/2001_CAF_Champions_League "2001 CAF Champions League"), the CAF introduced the semi-final stage after group stage, then the top two teams in each group would meet in the semi-finals, with the winners going through to contest the final.

Beginning with [the 2009 season](/wiki/2009_CAF_Champions_League "2009 CAF Champions League"), the prize money increased to $1.5 million for the champions and $1 million for the runner-ups. Since the competition rebranded in 1997, teams from North Africa have come to dominate the competition and its records. Morocco's [Raja Casablanca](/wiki/Raja_CA "Raja CA") won two of the first three editions,[[13]](#cite_note-13) but Al Ahly became the most successful team, winning the [2001](/wiki/2001_CAF_Champions_League "2001 CAF Champions League"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) [2005](/wiki/2005_CAF_Champions_League "2005 CAF Champions League"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) [2006](/wiki/2006_CAF_Champions_League "2006 CAF Champions League"),[[16]](#cite_note-16) [2008](/wiki/2008_CAF_Champions_League "2008 CAF Champions League")[[17]](#cite_note-17) and [2012](/wiki/2012_CAF_Champions_League "2012 CAF Champions League") editions,[[18]](#cite_note-18) while [Zamalek](/wiki/Zamalek_SC "Zamalek SC") managed to be champions in [2002](/wiki/2002_CAF_Champions_League "2002 CAF Champions League").[[19]](#cite_note-19) Tunisian teams broke into the winners' circle with [Étoile du Sahel](/wiki/%C3%89toile_Sportive_du_Sahel "Étoile Sportive du Sahel") winning [the 2007 edition](/wiki/2007_CAF_Champions_League "2007 CAF Champions League") after being a losing finalist in [2004](/wiki/2004_CAF_Champions_League "2004 CAF Champions League") and [2005](/wiki/2005_CAF_Champions_League "2005 CAF Champions League").[[20]](#cite_note-20) For its part, [Espérance de Tunis](/wiki/Esp%C3%A9rance_Sportive_de_Tunis "Espérance Sportive de Tunis") achieved its second continental title in [2011](/wiki/2011_CAF_Champions_League "2011 CAF Champions League") after having lost in the finals in the [1999](/wiki/1999_CAF_Champions_League "1999 CAF Champions League"), [2000](/wiki/2000_CAF_Champions_League "2000 CAF Champions League"), [2010](/wiki/2010_CAF_Champions_League "2010 CAF Champions League") and [2012](/wiki/2012_CAF_Champions_League "2012 CAF Champions League") editions.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Despite the clear dominance of North African teams, Nigerian club [Enyimba](/wiki/Enyimba_F.C. "Enyimba F.C.") won their first two titles back-to-back in [2003](/wiki/2003_CAF_Champions_League "2003 CAF Champions League") and [2004](/wiki/2004_CAF_Champions_League "2004 CAF Champions League").[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) [ASEC Mimosas](/wiki/ASEC_Mimosas "ASEC Mimosas") from Ivory Coast and [Accra Hearts of Oak](/wiki/Accra_Hearts_of_Oak_S.C. "Accra Hearts of Oak S.C.") from Ghana added two championships for West Africa. In [2010](/wiki/2010_CAF_Champions_League "2010 CAF Champions League"), [TP Mazembe](/wiki/TP_Mazembe "TP Mazembe") from [the DRC](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo") became the first club to repeat as champions on two occasions, with the first pair of wins arriving in [1967](/wiki/1967_African_Cup_of_Champions_Clubs "1967 African Cup of Champions Clubs") and [1968](/wiki/1968_African_Cup_of_Champions_Clubs "1968 African Cup of Champions Clubs"),[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) before repeating the feat again in [2009](/wiki/2009_CAF_Champions_League "2009 CAF Champions League") and [2010](/wiki/2010_CAF_Champions_League "2010 CAF Champions League").[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) In 2017, the group phase was expanded from 2 groups of 4 teams to 4 groups of 16, with the automatic addition of the quarter-finals stage.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Esp%C3%A9rance_Sportive_de_Tunis_wins_CAF_Champions_League.jpg/330px-Esp%C3%A9rance_Sportive_de_Tunis_wins_CAF_Champions_League.jpg)](/wiki/File:Esp%C3%A9rance_Sportive_de_Tunis_wins_CAF_Champions_League.jpg)

Espérance Sportive players celebrating the 2018 CAF Champions League title.

The [2020–21 season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_CAF_Champions_League "2020–21 CAF Champions League") was played behind closed doors due to the [COVID-19 pandemic in Africa](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Africa "COVID-19 pandemic in Africa") in line with [global football leagues and competitions](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_association_football "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on association football"). Nevertheless, Al Ahly faced [bitter rivals](/wiki/Sports_rivalry "Sports rivalry") [Zamalek](/wiki/Zamalek_SC "Zamalek SC") in [an-all Egyptian final](/wiki/2020_CAF_Champions_League_final "2020 CAF Champions League final") (the first time two clubs from the same country compete in any final in the competition's history),[[31]](#cite_note-31) with the former emerging victorious and winning its ninth title.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Al Ahly [successfully defended their title for a record-extending 10th time](/wiki/2021_CAF_Champions_League_final "2021 CAF Champions League final") the following season by beating 10-men [Kaizer Chiefs](/wiki/Kaizer_Chiefs_F.C. "Kaizer Chiefs F.C.") of South Africa,[[33]](#cite_note-33) but were unable to secure a 3rd consecutive title in a row and 11th title [in 2022](/wiki/2022_CAF_Champions_League_final "2022 CAF Champions League final") as they were defeated 2–0 by Moroccan club [Wydad AC](/wiki/Wydad_AC "Wydad AC") who instead captured their 3rd title.[[34]](#cite_note-34) With a return to [two-legged](/wiki/Two-legged_tie "Two-legged tie") finals after a 24-month hiatus owing to the pandemic, Al Ahly roared back, got their revenge [the following season](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_CAF_Champions_League "2022–23 CAF Champions League") and wrestled the title back from Wydad, thus claiming their 11th title [in 2023](/wiki/2023_CAF_Champions_League_final "2023 CAF Champions League final") with a 3–2 aggregate win thanks to forward Mohamed Abdelmoneim's tie-breaking goal[[35]](#cite_note-35) and successfully defended it for the second time in the space of half a decade (5 years) [in 2024](/wiki/2024_CAF_Champions_League_final "2024 CAF Champions League final") for a record extending 12th title with a 1–0 aggregate win over Tunisia's Esperance.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

With the introduction of the [Africa Football League](/wiki/Africa_Football_League "Africa Football League") in the 2023–24 season, CAF attempted to establish a new competition to rival the CAF Champions League.[[37]](#cite_note-AfFL-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) However, the Africa Football League failed to generate the same level of popular enthusiasm and only lasted for a single edition. The CAF Champions League remains Africa's premier club competition, boasting the highest prize money on the continent.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Structure and qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Champions_League&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Structure and qualification")]

### Qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Champions_League&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Qualification")]

The CAF Champions League is open to the winners of all CAF-affiliated national leagues, as well as the title holders from the previous season. From [the 2004 season](/wiki/2004_CAF_Champions_League "2004 CAF Champions League") onward, with the merging of the [CAF Cup](/wiki/CAF_Cup "CAF Cup") and the [African Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/African_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "African Cup Winners' Cup") to create the second-tier [CAF Confederation Cup](/wiki/CAF_Confederation_Cup "CAF Confederation Cup"), the runners-up of football leagues of [the 12 highest-ranked countries](/wiki/CAF_5-year_ranking "CAF 5-year ranking") also enter the tournament, making up a total of 64 in-competition teams. The 12 countries would be ranked based on the performance of their clubs in the previous 5 seasons/editions of the competition (the plain definition of the *[CAF 5-year ranking](/wiki/CAF_5-year_ranking "CAF 5-year ranking")*).[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The number of teams that each association enters into the CAF Champions League is determined annually through criteria as set by the CAF Competitions Committee.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) The higher an association's ranking as determined by the criteria, the more teams represent the association in the Champions League, and the fewer qualification rounds the association's teams must compete in.

The CAF Champions League operates primarily as a knockout competition, with trim-down qualification rounds, a group stage, a two-legged knockout stage and a two-legged final. At the start of the competition, the 64 qualified teams enter 2 qualification rounds: the preliminary stage and the first round. After the first qualifying round, the remaining teams are split into four groups of 4, whereas the teams each first-round winner vanquished transfer to the second qualification round of the Confederation Cup for hopes of group stage progression. The winners and runners-up of each group progress to the [two-legged](/wiki/Two-legged_tie "Two-legged tie") knockout stage for hopes of progression to a two-legged final for a chance to lift the trophy for their member association.

## Sponsorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Champions_League&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Sponsorship")]

In October 2004, [MTN](/wiki/MTN_Group "MTN Group") contracted a four-year deal to sponsor CAF's competitions worth US$12.5 million, which at that time was the biggest sponsorship deal in African sporting history.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In 2008, CAF put a value of [€](/wiki/Euro "Euro")100 million for a comprehensive and long-term package of its competitions when it opened tenders for a new sponsor, which was scooped up by French telecommunications giant [Orange](/wiki/Orange_S.A. "Orange S.A.") through the signing of an eight-year deal the following year in July, whose terms were not disclosed.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

On 21 July 2016, French energy and petroleum giant, [TotalEnergies](/wiki/TotalEnergies "TotalEnergies")[[44]](#cite_note-44) (at the time known as *Total S.A.*) secured an 8-year sponsorship package from CAF to support its competitions, including its main competition, the [Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Africa Cup of Nations").[[45]](#cite_note-TotalEnergies-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

## Prizes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Champions_League&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Prizes")]

### Trophy and medals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CAF_Champions_League&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Trophy and medals")]

Each year, the winning team is presented with the CAF Champions League, the current version of which has been awarded since the competition name change in 1997. Forty gold medals are presented to the competition winners and 40 silver medals to the runners-up. On May 22, 2025, CAF unveiled a striking new design for the TotalEnergies CAF Champions League trophy at TotalEnergies' Johannesburg headquarters.[[56]](#cite_note-:0-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) Crafted in sleek silver with bold gold accents, the trophy features a golden sphere adorned with African motifs at its peak—symbolizing the ultimate triumph—while the alternating silver and gold lines reflect unity, rivalry, and competitive balance that define Africa’s premier club competition.[[56]](#cite_note-:0-56)[[58]](#cite_note-58) The update is part of CAF’s broader effort to modernize its competitions and enhance their appeal.
